   516→```md
   517→# Plan: Optimize CLAUDE.md with Progressive Disclosure
   518→
   519→## Context
   520→
   521→`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is symlinked from this repo (`~/.claude-shared-config/CLAUDE.md`). It's ~2200 words / ~4.5k tokens loaded into **every** conversation. Most of that content is situational — Java testing standards don't matter when writing Terraform, the Gemini CLI reference isn't needed unless you're doing large codebase analysis, and the full org navigation map is only needed when switching between projects.
   522→
   523→**Goal:** Reduce the always-loaded CLAUDE.md to ~800-1000 words (~2k tokens) by extracting situational content into files that Claude loads on demand via `Read` when the context calls for it.
   524→
   525→## What stays in CLAUDE.md (always-loaded)
   526→
   527→These are universal rules that apply to virtually every session:
   528→
   529→- **Configurations** — load local AGENTS.md/GEMINI.md (2 lines, keep as-is)
   530→- **Organizations summary** — compressed to ~5 lines: just org names, roots, and a pointer to the full map file. The key insight: Claude just needs to know orgs exist and where to look, not every sub-path.
   531→- **Core behavioral rules** — no sycophancy, read README before running, sequential git commands (compress to ~5 lines, drop the verbose example)
   532→- **Development Workflow** — branch hygiene, feature flags, don't commit docs, don't touch unfamiliar code (keep as-is, ~10 lines, universal)
   533→- **Shipping safeguards** — the 4 "NEVER do X from Claude" lines (keep, they're safety rails)
   534→- **Code Style Preferences** — compressed to the 3 key principles (self-documenting code, small methods, no comment cruft) in ~5 lines. Drop the Java-specific Mockito details.
   535→
   536→**Estimated always-loaded size: ~50-60 lines / ~1000 words / ~2k tokens** (55% reduction)
   537→
   538→## What gets extracted into `~/.claude/context/` files
   539→
   540→Each file gets a clear trigger description in CLAUDE.md so Claude knows when to load it.
   541→
   542→### 1. `workspace-map.yaml` — Organization & project navigation
   543→- **Contains:** Full org tree (Klever paths, Supervisr paths, Origin8, personal, ticket conventions, project workspaces)
   544→- **Trigger in CLAUDE.md:** _"When navigating between projects, starting work on a ticket, or when user mentions a specific org/project, read `~/.claude/context/workspace-map.yaml`"_
   545→- **Lines saved:** ~50
   546→
   547→### 2. `shipping-workflow.md` — Tagging, deployment, branch conventions
   548→- **Contains:** App repo tagging (pre-tag checklist, versioning, JIB), DAC/Terraform repo rules, branch conventions
   549→- **Trigger in CLAUDE.md:** _"When tagging, shipping, deploying, creating merge requests, or working with CI/CD, read `~/.claude/context/shipping-workflow.md`"_
   550→- **Lines saved:** ~35
   551→
   552→### 3. `java-standards.md` — Java code style & testing
   553→- **Contains:** Mockito strictness rules, testing standards (constants, naming, structure), the "why best practices matter" lesson
   554→- **Trigger in CLAUDE.md:** _"When writing or reviewing Java code, read `~/.claude/context/java-standards.md`"_
   555→- **Lines saved:** ~55
   556→
   557→### 4. `gemini-cli-reference.md` — Gemini CLI usage
   558→- **Contains:** Full `gemini -p` syntax, examples, when-to-use guide
   559→- **Trigger in CLAUDE.md:** _"When user asks to use Gemini CLI or when analyzing codebases too large for context, read `~/.claude/context/gemini-cli-reference.md`"_
   560→- **Lines saved:** ~75
   561→
   562→### 5. `tools-catalog.md` — Project management & documentation tools
   563→- **Contains:** Tool paths (create-jira-ticket, create-prd, generate-tasks, update-changelog, ADR templates)
   564→- **Trigger in CLAUDE.md:** _"When creating PRDs, tickets, development contracts, changelogs, or ADRs, read `~/.claude/context/tools-catalog.md`"_
   565→- **Lines saved:** ~20
   566→
   567→## Files to modify/create
   568→
   569→| File | Action |
   570→|------|--------|
   571→| `CLAUDE.md` | Rewrite — keep core rules + trigger pointers |
   572→| `~/.claude/context/workspace-map.yaml` | Create — org navigation tree |
   573→| `~/.claude/context/shipping-workflow.md` | Create — tagging & deployment |
   574→| `~/.claude/context/java-standards.md` | Create — Java style + testing |
   575→| `~/.claude/context/gemini-cli-reference.md` | Create — Gemini CLI docs |
   576→| `~/.claude/context/tools-catalog.md` | Create — tool paths & descriptions |
   577→
   578→Since CLAUDE.md is symlinked, the actual edit target is `/Users/gabrielamyot/.claude-shared-config/CLAUDE.md`, and the context files go in `/Users/gabrielamyot/.claude-shared-config/context/` (we may need to symlink `~/.claude/context/` → this directory as well).
   579→
   580→## Verification
   581→
   582→1. Check symlink still works: `ls -la ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`
   583→2. Count tokens in new CLAUDE.md — should be ~2k (down from ~4.5k)
   584→3. Manually test a few scenarios:
   585→   - Start a Java coding session → Claude should load `java-standards.md` when writing tests
   586→   - Ask to tag a service → Claude should load `shipping-workflow.md`
   587→   - Start work on a Klever ticket → Claude should load `workspace-map.yaml`
   588→4. Verify context files are readable: `cat ~/.claude/context/*.md ~/.claude/context/*.yaml`

   188→    86→## Gemini Added Memories
   189→    87→- Never pipe git commands. Do them sequentially
   190→    88→	- Example:
   191→    89→		- Dont:
   192→    90→				> git fetch origin && git status && git log -n 1 main
   193→    91→		- do instead:
   194→    92→				> git fetch
   195→    93→				> git status
   196→    94→				> git log -n 1 main
   197→    95→
   198→    96→- Before running a project, I should read the README.md file to learn how to execute it.
   199→    97→- Sycophancy is prohibited. Be critical and constructive when answering user's questions/requests.
   200→    98→
   201→    99→## Development Workflow
   202→   100→- Before starting a feature or bug fix in a git repo:
   203→   101→	- Check if the branch is clean and up to date
   204→   102→	- If on a feature branch (not dev/main/master), recommend switching back to the main branch (contextual: some repos use `dev`, most use `main` or `master`)
   205→   103→	- Propose: switch to main branch → pull origin → create a new branch for the fix/feature
   206→   104→- Assume feature flags will be used for any complex feature implementation
   207→   105→	- Wire up the feature flag from the start, with fallback to legacy behavior when disabled
   208→   106→	- This enables safe rollout and easy rollback
   209→   107→- **NEVER commit documentation to repo** - put implementation summaries, design docs, analysis in `project-management/tickets/{ticket-or-branch-name}/`
   210→   108→- **Don't touch what you don't understand.** If you see something unfamiliar in the codebase (a config field, an input variable, a file you didn't create), do NOT delete or modify it. Ask the user first. Absence of understanding is not permission to remove.
   211→   109→- **You don't own other people's code.** Other engineers contribute to these repos. Respect their work. If something looks wrong but was added by someone else, flag it — don't silently change it.
   212→   110→
   213→   111→## Tagging & Shipping Workflow
   214→   112→
   215→   113→### App Repos (micro-services/*)
   216→   114→- **Pre-tag checklist** (mandatory before every tag):
   217→   115→	1. Detect primary branch: `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` → extract name. Fallback: REPO_MAPPING `main_branch`, or convention (apps=`main`).
   218→   116→	2. `git fetch origin`
   219→   117→	3. `git rebase origin/{primary-branch}` — abort and alert user on conflicts.
   220→   118→	4. Get latest tag: `git tag --sort=-v:refname | head -1`
   221→   119→	5. Only then create the new tag.
   222→   120→- **Tag versioning:**
   223→   121→	- Format: `x.y.z-dev` (dev/untested) or `x.y.z` (prod-ready).
   224→   122→	- Default: always tag `-dev`. Increment patch from last tag (e.g., `0.0.4-dev` → `0.0.5-dev`).
   225→   123→	- Prod promotion: only when user explicitly says a `-dev` tag is tested and prod-ready. Drop `-dev` suffix, same patch (e.g., `0.0.5-dev` → `0.0.5`). This is rare — `-dev` tags in prod is normal and the default.
   226→   124→	- No tags yet → start at `0.0.1-dev`.
   227→   125→- **Image build (JIB):**
   228→   126→	- **All Java services use JIB** (Maven plugin `jib-maven-plugin`). No Dockerfiles for deployment.
   229→   127→	- Build and push command: `mvn compile jib:build -Djib.to.tags=X.Y.Z-dev -DskipTests`
   230→   128→	- JIB pushes directly to Artifact Registry — no manual `docker build`/`docker push`.
   231→   129→	- The app tag and the image tag must match (e.g., git tag `0.0.6-dev` → image `service-name:0.0.6-dev`).
   232→   130→
   233→   131→### DAC/Terraform Repos (dac-sprvsr-core-*)
   234→   132→- **NEVER create git tags on DAC repos.** DAC repos do not use git tags.
   235→   133→- "Tag" in DAC context means the **CI/CD variable `TF_VAR_IMAGE_TAG`** in GitLab. This controls which Docker image version the Cloud Run service deploys.
   236→   134→- DAC deployment workflow: update `TF_VAR_IMAGE_TAG` in GitLab → trigger pipeline on `dev`.
   237→   135→- **NEVER commit directly to `dev`, `uat`, or `main`.** Always create a branch named after the ticket (e.g., `SPV-4`), then create a merge request.
   238→   136→- **NEVER modify `README.md`** in DAC repos — it is auto-generated by terraform-docs. Use `README_local.md` (gitignored) for local notes.
   239→   137→
   240→   138→### Branch conventions
   241→   139→- DAC/Terraform repos (`dac-sprvsr-core-*`): primary = `dev`. Also have `uat` and `main` for promotion.
   242→   140→- App repos (`micro-services/*`): primary = `main`. Always verify via `origin/HEAD`.
   243→   141→- Branch naming: use ticket ID (e.g., `SPV-4`), not `feature/` prefix.
   244→   142→
   245→   143→### Shipping safeguards
   246→   144→- Do NOT run GitLab pipelines from Claude
   247→   145→- Do NOT update GitLab CI/CD variables from Claude
   248→   146→- Do NOT run terraform plan/apply
   249→   147→- Commits and tags push; CI/CD picks up automatically
   250→   148→
   251→   149→## Code Style Preferences
   252→   150→- Code should be self-documenting. Avoid comments that explain what the code does (e.g., `// Check if feature flag is enabled`). Instead:
   253→   151→	- Name variables and methods intuitively so the code reads naturally
   254→   152→	- If explanation is truly needed, use `log.debug()` messages instead of comments - this transforms static comments into live, observable code
   255→   153→- **Keep methods small and focused** - extract helper methods instead of writing long methods with block comments:
   256→   154→	- If you're about to write a comment like `// Step 3: Validate input`, extract that block into `validateInput()` instead
   257→   155→	- A well-named method is better than a comment - let the method name document the intent
   258→   156→	- Boolean helpers should read like questions: `isStaleWebhook()`, `hasPermission()`, `shouldRetry()`
   259→   157→	- This reduces cognitive load and improves maintainability
   260→   158→- In Java tests, avoid `@MockitoSettings(strictness = Strictness.LENIENT)` at the class level. Instead:
   261→   159→	- Set up only the stubs each test actually needs
   262→   160→	- Use helper methods (e.g., `stubSyncDependencies()`) for common stubs shared by multiple tests
   263→   161→	- Use `lenient().when()` sparingly for specific stubs that may not always be used
   264→   162→- Keep tests compact - remove unnecessary empty lines between Given/When/Then blocks
   265→   163→
   266→   164→## Testing Standards
   267→   165→- **Extract test constants:** Use `private static final` fields for constants reused across multiple tests (IDs, dates, values)
   268→   166→- **Use List.of():** Never use `Arrays.asList()` or `new ArrayList<>()` in tests
   269→   167→- **Test naming:** Follow `{Given}{When}{Then}` pattern (e.g., `validJobId_whenGetJobCalled_thenReturnsJob`)
   270→   168→- **One behavior per test:** Each test method verifies exactly one behavior or condition
   271→   169→- **//given //when //then:** Structure test body with comments as mental separators, no extra blank lines between them
   272→   170→  ```java
   273→   171→  //given
   274→   172→  when(service.method()).thenReturn(value);
   275→   173→  //when
   276→   174→  var result = controller.call();
   277→   175→  //then
   278→   176→  assertEquals(expected, result);
   279→   177→  ```
   280→   178→- **Tight setup:** No blank lines between `@Mock`, `@MockBean`, field declarations and `@Before`/`setUp()` at class top
   281→   179→- **Helper methods:** Use private helper methods (e.g., `stubValidationPasses()`) for common stub setup
   282→   180→- **Meaningful names:** Use constant names like `VALID_JOB_ID`, `ADVERTISER_90` instead of magic values
   283→   181→- **Test method naming:** Follow `{Given}{When}{Then}` pattern (e.g., `campaignOnly_whenGetLevel_thenReturnsLevel`)
   284→   182→  - Not: `getCurrentDspReportLevel_shouldSelectMostGranular` (too vague)
   285→   183→  - Yes: `campaignOnly_whenGetCurrentDspReportLevel_thenReturnsCampaignLevel` (crystal clear)
   286→   184→- **No comment cruft in tests:** Remove explanatory comments like `// Campaign only → campaigns table`
   287→   185→  - The test name + variable names should make it obvious
   288→   186→  - Refactor test names to be self-documenting instead
   289→   187→
   290→   188→## Critical: Why Best Practices Enforcement Matters
   291→   189→
   292→   190→**Lesson Learned:** Initial implementation of DspReportLevelTest violated these standards (missing constants, verbose comments, unclear naming). Later feedback exposed the violations.
   293→   191→
   294→   192→**Why This Matters:**
   295→   193→1. **Maintainability:** Hardcoded strings make refactoring dangerous (typos, inconsistencies)
   296→   194→2. **Single Source of Truth:** Constants prevent divergence between enums and test strings
   297→   195→3. **Readability:** Clear {Given}{When}{Then} naming makes tests self-documenting
   298→   196→4. **Consistency:** Enforced standards make code predictable across the codebase
   299→   197→5. **Prevention:** Violations caught now don't compound into future technical debt
   300→   198→
   301→   199→**Prevention Strategy:**
   302→   200→- Review tests BEFORE approving PRs for:
   303→   201→  - ✓ String constants extracted (no hardcoded dimension names, table names, etc.)
   304→   202→  - ✓ Test names follow {Given}{When}{Then} pattern
   305→   203→  - ✓ No explanatory comments (naming should suffice)
   306→   204→  - ✓ One behavior per test method
   307→   205→  - ✓ All values extracted as class constants
   308→   206→- Use linters/IDE inspections to catch repeated strings automatically
   309→   207→- Document standards in local CLAUDE.md (specific to service)
   310→   208→- Don't assume "we'll clean it up later"—enforce from the start
   311→   209→
   312→   210→# Using Gemini CLI for Large Codebase Analysis
   313→   211→
   314→   212→  When analyzing large codebases or multiple files that might exceed context limits, use the Gemini CLI with its massive
   315→   213→  context window. Use `gemini -p` to leverage Google Gemini's large context capacity.
   316→   214→
   317→   215→  ## File and Directory Inclusion Syntax
   318→   216→
   319→   217→  Use the `@` syntax to include files and directories in your Gemini prompts. The paths should be relative to WHERE you run the
   320→   218→   gemini command:
   321→   219→
   322→   220→  ### Examples:
   323→   221→
   324→   222→  **Single file analysis:**
   325→   223→  ```bash
   326→   224→  gemini -p "@src/main.py Explain this file's purpose and structure"
   327→   225→
   328→   226→  Multiple files:
   329→   227→  gemini -p "@package.json @src/index.js Analyze the dependencies used in the code"
   330→   228→
   331→   229→  Entire directory:
   332→   230→  gemini -p "@src/ Summarize the architecture of this codebase"
   333→   231→
   334→   232→  Multiple directories:
   335→   233→  gemini -p "@src/ @tests/ Analyze test coverage for the source code"
   336→   234→
   337→   235→  Current directory and subdirectories:
   338→   236→  gemini -p "@./ Give me an overview of this entire project"
   339→   237→  
   340→   238→#
   341→   239→ Or use --all_files flag:
   342→   240→  gemini --all_files -p "Analyze the project structure and dependencies"
   343→   241→
   344→   242→  Implementation Verification Examples
   345→   243→
   346→   244→  Check if a feature is implemented:
   347→   245→  gemini -p "@src/ @lib/ Has dark mode been implemented in this codebase? Show me the relevant files and functions"
   348→   246→
   349→   247→  Verify authentication implementation:
   350→   248→  gemini -p "@src/ @middleware/ Is JWT authentication implemented? List all auth-related endpoints and middleware"
   351→   249→
   352→   250→  Check for specific patterns:
   353→   251→  gemini -p "@src/ Are there any React hooks that handle WebSocket connections? List them with file paths"
   354→   252→
   355→   253→  Verify error handling:
   356→   254→  gemini -p "@src/ @api/ Is proper error handling implemented for all API endpoints? Show examples of try-catch blocks"
   357→   255→
   358→   256→  Check for rate limiting:
   359→   257→  gemini -p "@backend/ @middleware/ Is rate limiting implemented for the API? Show the implementation details"
   360→   258→
   361→   259→  Verify caching strategy:
   362→   260→  gemini -p "@src/ @lib/ @services/ Is Redis caching implemented? List all cache-related functions and their usage"
   363→   261→
   364→   262→  Check for specific security measures:
   365→   263→  gemini -p "@src/ @api/ Are SQL injection protections implemented? Show how user inputs are sanitized"
   366→   264→
   367→   265→  Verify test coverage for features:
   368→   266→  gemini -p "@src/payment/ @tests/ Is the payment processing module fully tested? List all test cases"
   369→   267→
   370→   268→  When to Use Gemini CLI
   371→   269→
   372→   270→  Use gemini -p when:
   373→   271→  - Analyzing entire codebases or large directories
   374→   272→  - Comparing multiple large files
   375→   273→  - Need to understand project-wide patterns or architecture
   376→   274→  - Current context window is insufficient for the task
   377→   275→  - Working with files totaling more than 100KB
   378→   276→  - Verifying if specific features, patterns, or security measures are implemented
   379→   277→  - Checking for the presence of certain coding patterns across the entire codebase
   380→   278→
   381→   279→  Important Notes
   382→   280→
   383→   281→  - Paths in @ syntax are relative to your current working directory when invoking gemini
   384→   282→  - The CLI will include file contents directly in the context
   385→   283→  - No need for --yolo flag for read-only analysis
   386→   284→  - Gemini's context window can handle entire codebases that would overflow Claude's context
   387→   285→  - When checking implementations, be specific about what you're looking for to get accurate results
   388→   286→
   389→   287→## PR Review with supervisr-github-review Agent
   390→   288→
   391→   289→When using the `supervisr-github-review` subagent to review PRs:
   392→   290→
   393→   291→### Draft-only — NEVER post comments to GitHub
   394→   292→- The agent must NEVER post comments, reviews, or reactions to PRs
   395→   293→- Do NOT include instructions about posting in the agent prompt
   396→   294→- The agent drafts comments → user reviews → user posts manually via GitHub UI
   397→   295→
   398→   296→### Comment format
   399→   297→Each draft comment must include:
   400→   298→1. **File and line(s)**: e.g., `SupervisorsDatafetcher.java:39`
   401→   299→2. **Comment text**: charitable, question-based (see phrasing rules below)
   402→   300→3. **GitHub suggestion block**: a fenced code block with the `suggestion` tag showing the proposed fix, ready to paste into GitHub's review UI:
   403→   301→   ````
   404→   302→   ```suggestion
   405→   303→   proposed code here
   406→   304→   ```
   407→   305→   ````
   408→   306→
   409→   307→### Phrasing rules — natural variation required
   410→   308→- Vary sentence openers across comments. Do NOT start every comment the same way
   411→   309→- Use synonyms and varied structures. Examples of openers (rotate, don't repeat):
   412→   310→  - "I notice…"
   413→   311→  - "Looking at this…"
   414→   312→  - "Quick question on…"
   415→   313→  - "This caught my eye…"
   416→   314→  - "Curious about…"
   417→   315→  - "Something worth checking…"
   418→   316→- Similarly vary closers: "Was this intentional or a miss?", "Is this the intended behavior?", "Worth a double-check?", "Thoughts?"
   419→   317→- The goal is to sound like a human reviewer, not a template
   420→   318→
   421→   319→### Scope — high-impact only
   422→   320→- Only flag bugs, logic errors, schema mismatches, security issues
   423→   321→- Skip style nits, formatting, naming preferences
   424→   322→- Assume good intent — frame as questions, not accusations
   425→```
   426→</details></tool-use>
   427→

   540→```md
   541→# Tagging & Shipping Workflow
   542→
   543→## App Repos (micro-services/*)
   544→
   545→### Pre-tag checklist (mandatory before every tag)
   546→1. Detect primary branch: `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` → extract name. Fallback: REPO_MAPPING `main_branch`, or convention (apps=`main`).
   547→2. `git fetch origin`
   548→3. `git rebase origin/{primary-branch}` — abort and alert user on conflicts.
   549→4. Get latest tag: `git tag --sort=-v:refname | head -1`
   550→5. Only then create the new tag.
   551→
   552→### Tag versioning
   553→- Format: `x.y.z-dev` (dev/untested) or `x.y.z` (prod-ready).
   554→- Default: always tag `-dev`. Increment patch from last tag (e.g., `0.0.4-dev` → `0.0.5-dev`).
   555→- Prod promotion: only when user explicitly says a `-dev` tag is tested and prod-ready. Drop `-dev` suffix, same patch (e.g., `0.0.5-dev` → `0.0.5`). This is rare — `-dev` tags in prod is normal and the default.
   556→- No tags yet → start at `0.0.1-dev`.
   557→
   558→### Image build (JIB)
   559→- **All Java services use JIB** (Maven plugin `jib-maven-plugin`). No Dockerfiles for deployment.
   560→- Build and push command: `mvn compile jib:build -Djib.to.tags=X.Y.Z-dev -DskipTests`
   561→- JIB pushes directly to Artifact Registry — no manual `docker build`/`docker push`.
   562→- The app tag and the image tag must match (e.g., git tag `0.0.6-dev` → image `service-name:0.0.6-dev`).
   563→
   564→## DAC/Terraform Repos (dac-sprvsr-core-*)
   565→- **NEVER create git tags on DAC repos.** DAC repos do not use git tags.
   566→- "Tag" in DAC context means the **CI/CD variable `TF_VAR_IMAGE_TAG`** in GitLab. This controls which Docker image version the Cloud Run service deploys.
   567→- DAC deployment workflow: update `TF_VAR_IMAGE_TAG` in GitLab → trigger pipeline on `dev`.
   568→- **NEVER commit directly to `dev`, `uat`, or `main`.** Always create a branch named after the ticket (e.g., `SPV-4`), then create a merge request.
   569→- **NEVER modify `README.md`** in DAC repos — it is auto-generated by terraform-docs. Use `README_local.md` (gitignored) for local notes.
   570→
   571→## Branch conventions
   572→- DAC/Terraform repos (`dac-sprvsr-core-*`): primary = `dev`. Also have `uat` and `main` for promotion.
   573→- App repos (`micro-services/*`): primary = `main`. Always verify via `origin/HEAD`.
   574→- Branch naming: use ticket ID (e.g., `SPV-4`), not `feature/` prefix.
   575→
   576→## PR Review with supervisr-github-review Agent
   577→
   578→When using the `supervisr-github-review` subagent to review PRs:
   579→
   580→### Draft-only — NEVER post comments to GitHub
   581→- The agent must NEVER post comments, reviews, or reactions to PRs
   582→- Do NOT include instructions about posting in the agent prompt
   583→- The agent drafts comments → user reviews → user posts manually via GitHub UI
   584→
   585→### Comment format
   586→Each draft comment must include:
   587→1. **File and line(s)**: e.g., `SupervisorsDatafetcher.java:39`
   588→2. **Comment text**: charitable, question-based (see phrasing rules below)
   589→3. **GitHub suggestion block**: a fenced code block with the `suggestion` tag showing the proposed fix
   590→
   591→### Phrasing rules — natural variation required
   592→- Vary sentence openers across comments. Do NOT start every comment the same way
   593→- Use synonyms and varied structures: "I notice…", "Looking at this…", "Quick question on…", "This caught my eye…", "Curious about…", "Something worth checking…"
   594→- Similarly vary closers: "Was this intentional or a miss?", "Is this the intended behavior?", "Worth a double-check?", "Thoughts?"
   595→- The goal is to sound like a human reviewer, not a template
   596→
   597→### Scope — high-impact only
   598→- Only flag bugs, logic errors, schema mismatches, security issues
   599→- Skip style nits, formatting, naming preferences
   600→- Assume good intent — frame as questions, not accusations
```

```text
# Supervisr.AI Engineering Handbook: CLAUDE.md

This document provides engineering guidance for the Supervisr.AI platform, including architecture, best practices, and configuration standards.

## 1. Project Overview

Supervisr.AI is a call analytics and compliance monitoring platform built on Google Cloud Platform. The system processes call interactions, applies compliance rules, and provides insights through a GraphQL API.

## 2. Architecture

- **Microservices**: Spring Boot services deployed on Cloud Run
- **API Layer**: GraphQL with Netflix DGS framework
- **Data Storage**: Google Cloud Datastore for materialized views
- **Event Processing**: Pub/Sub for async communication
- **Authentication**: Auth0 / OAuth2 JWT tokens

## 3. Tech Stack

### Backend Services
- **Language**: Java 17
- **Framework**: Spring Boot 3.x
- **GraphQL**: Netflix DGS 8.x
- **Cloud**: Spring Cloud GCP 5.x
- **Build**: Apache Maven

### Libraries
- **event-query-framework**: Core Datastore query framework
- **Lombok**: Reduce boilerplate
- **Jackson**: JSON processing

## 4. Maven Artifact Registry Configuration (Multi-Region Infrastructure)

As of January 2025, all Maven artifacts are hosted in GCP project `prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95` in the `us-east1` region.

### Repository Configuration (For Pulling Dependencies)

All `pom.xml` files should use the **Virtual Repository** for pulling dependencies:

```xml
<repositories>
    <repository>
        <id>o8-internal-virtual</id>
        <url>https://us-east1-maven.pkg.dev/prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95/are-usea1-maven-virtual-o8-internal</url>
        <releases><enabled>true</enabled></releases>
        <snapshots><enabled>true</enabled></snapshots>
    </repository>
</repositories>

<pluginRepositories>
    <pluginRepository>
        <id>o8-internal-virtual</id>
        <url>https://us-east1-maven.pkg.dev/prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95/are-usea1-maven-virtual-o8-internal</url>
        <releases><enabled>true</enabled></releases>
        <snapshots><enabled>true</enabled></snapshots>
    </pluginRepository>
</pluginRepositories>
```

### Distribution Management (For Publishing Artifacts)

Use **Standard Repositories** for pushing (NOT the virtual repository):

```xml
<distributionManagement>
    <repository>
        <id>o8-internal-release</id>
        <url>https://us-east1-maven.pkg.dev/prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95/are-usea1-maven-standard-o8-internal-release</url>
    </repository>
    <snapshotRepository>
        <id>o8-internal-snapshot</id>
        <url>https://us-east1-maven.pkg.dev/prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95/are-usea1-maven-standard-o8-internal-snapshot</url>
    </snapshotRepository>
</distributionManagement>
```

### Local Maven Settings (~/.m2/settings.xml)

Add server credentials for GCP authentication:

```xml
<servers>
    <server>
        <id>o8-internal-virtual</id>
        <configuration>
            <httpConfiguration>
                <get><usePreemptive>true</usePreemptive></get>
            </httpConfiguration>
        </configuration>
    </server>
    <server>
        <id>o8-internal-release</id>
        <configuration>
            <httpConfiguration>
                <get><usePreemptive>true</usePreemptive></get>
            </httpConfiguration>
        </configuration>
    </server>
    <server>
        <id>o8-internal-snapshot</id>
        <configuration>
            <httpConfiguration>
                <get><usePreemptive>true</usePreemptive></get>
            </httpConfiguration>
        </configuration>
    </server>
</servers>
```

### Required Build Extension

Ensure this extension is in your `pom.xml` for GCP Artifact Registry authentication:

```xml
<build>
    <extensions>
        <extension>
            <groupId>com.google.cloud.artifactregistry</groupId>
            <artifactId>artifactregistry-maven-wagon</artifactId>
            <version>2.2.0</version>
        </extension>
    </extensions>
</build>
```

### Verification

Test your configuration:
```bash
mvn clean deploy -DskipTests
```

## 5. Key Services

| Service | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `supervisor-query-service` | GraphQL API for querying interaction reports, rules, compliance configs |
| `web-ers` | Event receiver service for data ingestion |

## 6. Development Commands

```bash
# Build without tests
mvn clean compile

# Run tests
mvn test

# Run locally
mvn spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=local

# Deploy snapshot
mvn clean deploy -DskipTests

# Generate GraphQL types
mvn graphqlcodegen:generate
```

## 7. Environment Variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `DATASTORE_NAMESPACE` | Datastore namespace (e.g., `sprvsr_materialized_view`) |
| `GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT` | GCP project ID |
| `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE` | Spring profile (local, dev, uat, prod) |
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
# CLAUDE Context: Project Management Master Guide

## CRITICAL: File Placement Rules (AI Agents MUST Follow)

**NEVER create files in the project-management root directory.** The root contains only:
- `CLAUDE.md` (this file)
- `GEMINI.md` (Gemini config)
- `.repo-index.yaml` (service graph index — maps all services to DAC/IAC repos, generated by `/validate-repo-links reindex`)
- Subdirectories: `tickets/`, `documentation/`, `general/`, `archive/`, `tools/`

**Before creating ANY file, determine its ticket context:**
1. Identify which ticket (e.g., SPV-3, MVP-001) the work belongs to
2. Place the file inside `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/`
3. If the file is a long-lived reference (ADR, contract), place it in `documentation/`
4. If the file is a strategic/business artifact, place it in `general/`
5. If no ticket context exists yet, **create the ticket folder first**, then write the file inside it

**Mapping of document types to locations:**
| Document Type | Location |
|---|---|
| Architecture specs, design docs | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/architecture/` (draft) → `documentation/architecture/` (final) |
| Implementation plans, checklists | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/architecture/` |
| Implementation summaries, release notes | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/implementation/` |
| Review reports, adversarial findings | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/reviews/` |
| Spike summaries, completion reports | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/` or `tickets/{EPIC}/{SPIKE-ID}/reports/` |
| Status tracking, progress logs | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/status/` |
| Jira ticket specs, dependency logs | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/` (root level OK for specs/mapping files) |
| Jira local data (AC, metadata, comments) | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/jira/` (managed by `/jira` skill or `ticket-manager` agent) |
| Meeting notes | `general/meetings/` or `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/meeting_notes/` |
| ADRs | `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/architecture/adr/` (draft) → `documentation/architecture/adr/` (promoted) |
| Contracts / data models | `documentation/architecture/contracts/` |

**Reports Convention:**
All AI-generated reports, summaries, and tracking documents MUST go under `tickets/{TICKET-ID}/reports/` organized by type:
- `reports/architecture/` - Design docs, implementation plans, architecture specs
- `reports/implementation/` - Implementation summaries, release notes, deployment configs
- `reports/reviews/` - Code reviews, adversarial reviews, findings
- `reports/status/` - Progress logs, status snapshots, task summaries
- `reports/ship/` - Shipping pipeline reports (validation, review, release, deploy) — used by `supervisr-ship` agent

**If you are unsure where a file goes, ask the user. Do NOT default to the root.**

---

## Directory Structure & Philosophy

```
project-management/
├── CLAUDE.md             # THIS FILE - rules and structure (stays at root)
├── GEMINI.md             # Gemini config (stays at root)
├── tickets/              # Execution (time-bound work) - one folder per ticket/epic
│   ├── SPV-3/           # Example: Epic with sub-tickets (SPV-4..SPV-8, SPV-21..SPV-23)
│   │   ├── README.md
│   │   ├── REPO_MAPPING.yaml
│   │   ├── STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml  # Current state front loader for agents
│   │   ├── reports/     # All generated reports/summaries
│   │   │   ├── architecture/    # Design docs, plans, specs
│   │   │   ├── implementation/  # Implementation summaries, release notes
│   │   │   ├── reviews/         # Review findings, adversarial reports
│   │   │   ├── status/          # Progress logs, snapshots
│   │   │   └── ship/            # Shipping pipeline reports
│   │   ├── SPV-4/
│   │   │   ├── README.md
│   │   │   ├── jira/            # Local Jira data (ac.yaml, ticket.yaml, etc.)
│   │   │   └── reports/
│   │   └── SPV-8/
│   │       ├── README.md
│   │       ├── jira/            # Source of truth for AC-weighted completion
│   │       └── reports/
│   ├── MVP-epic/
│   │   ├── MVP-000/     # Planning: "What to build"
│   │   └── MVP-001/     # Implementation: "How to build"
│   └── ...
├── documentation/        # Knowledge Base (long-lived truth)
│   ├── architecture/
│   │   ├── adr/         # Architecture Decision Records
│   │   └── contracts/   # System interfaces & data models
│   ├── process/         # Extracted process guides (satellite docs)
│   └── tech_stack.md
├── general/              # Company Strategy (broad context, NOT ticket-specific)
│   └── meetings/         # Strategic meeting notes & DECISIONS_LOG.md
├── archive/              # Completed/deprecated work (date-stamped folders)
└── tools/                # Shared tooling configs
```

**Nothing else belongs at the root level. No loose .md files, no summaries, no reports.**

---

## Core Workflow (Summary)

| Phase | Input | Output | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capture | Meeting | Notes | `general/meetings/` or `tickets/{ID}/meeting_notes/` |
| Synthesize | Notes | Decisions | `DECISIONS_LOG.md` (appropriate scope) |
| Formalize | Decision | ADR | `tickets/{ID}/architecture/adr/` → `documentation/` |
| Define | Decision | Contract | `documentation/architecture/contracts/` |

**Need details?** → [Core Workflow Guide](documentation/process/core-workflow-guide.md) covers:
- How to write and update DECISIONS_LOG.md entries
- ADR template (MADR format) and naming convention
- Contract update process and alignment checks
- Multi-service deployment ordering (ERS → EQS → Lead Lifecycle → Retell)

---

## Promotion Rules (Ticket → Global)

**MUST DO before marking a ticket complete:**
1. **Move/Merge** ticket-local architecture artifacts:
   - `tickets/{ID}/architecture/adr/*.md` → `documentation/architecture/adr/`
   - `tickets/{ID}/architecture/contracts/*` → `documentation/architecture/contracts/`
2. **Update** global `DECISIONS_LOG.md` to reflect promoted changes
3. **Verify** documentation reflects actual implemented state

**Why?** Ticket folders are time-bound context. Global documentation is permanent truth.

---

## When to Create ADRs / Update Contracts

| Action | YES | NO |
|---|---|---|
| **Create ADR** | Chose between architectural patterns, selected core technology, changed fundamental data model, security/compliance decision | Implementation details, tactical code choices, bug fixes, minor refactoring |
| **Update Contracts** | GraphQL schema changes, API endpoints added/modified/removed, data entity fields change, frontend-backend interface shifts, compliance engine output format changes | Internal implementation changes that don't affect interfaces |

**Contract files:** `documentation/architecture/contracts/` (DATA_MODEL_AND_MAPPING.md, complianceEngine/*.json, frontEnd/*.json)

---

## Context Flow Rules

1. **Downward Flow:** Context flows from general → MVP-000 → MVP-001
2. **Constraints:** Implementation (MVP-001) respects scope from Planning (MVP-000)
3. **Upward Promotion:** Completed work promotes to documentation/
4. **Single Source of Truth:** `documentation/` is authoritative, tickets are drafts

---

## Ticket Initialization

New epics require: `README.md`, `REPO_MAPPING.yaml`, `STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml`, and `reports/` directory structure.
Templates in `/tools/templates/`.

**Need details?** → [Ticket Initialization Guide](documentation/process/ticket-initialization-guide.md) covers:
- Full directory scaffold with all subdirectories
- REPO_MAPPING.yaml: when to create, when to update, who uses it
- STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml: living vs. historical snapshots, content, archival workflow

---

## Agent Report Rules (Summary)

1. **Read front loaders FIRST:** `README.md` → `REPO_MAPPING.yaml` → `STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml`
2. All reports go under `tickets/{ID}/reports/{category}/`
3. Categories: `architecture/`, `implementation/`, `reviews/`, `status/`, `ship/`
4. Naming pattern: `[prefix-][context-][type]-[date].md` (kebab-case)
5. Ticket root-level files limited to: `README.md`, `REPO_MAPPING.yaml`, `STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml`, `DECISIONS_LOG.md`

**Need details?** → [Agent Report Output Standards](documentation/process/agent-report-output-standards.md) covers:
- File naming convention with component table and concrete examples
- Jira local folder structure (`jira/ac.yaml`, `ticket.yaml`, etc.)
- `/status-index` skill usage and AC-weighted completion algorithm
- Ticket root-level exceptions (full list)

---

## Working with This System

### Starting New Work
1. Read this file (`CLAUDE.md`)
2. Read ticket front loaders: `README.md` → `REPO_MAPPING.yaml` → `STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml`
3. Check `documentation/architecture/` for current system state
4. Read relevant meeting_notes/ and DECISIONS_LOG.md as needed

### During Work
1. **Update STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml** as progress is made
2. Update ticket-local DECISIONS_LOG or notes
3. Draft ADRs in ticket-local `architecture/adr/`
4. Write all reports to `reports/{category}/`

### Completing Work
1. Promote ADRs to global `documentation/`
2. Update contracts if interfaces changed
3. Sync global DECISIONS_LOG
4. Archive ticket context if needed

---

## Key Files Hierarchy

**Strategic (Business):**
- `general/meetings/DECISIONS_LOG.md` - Company direction

**Planning (Product):**
- `tickets/MVP-000/` - Product requirements

**Technical (Engineering):**
- `tickets/MVP-001/` - Active implementation
- `tickets/MVP-001/MVP-001-IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` - Living execution guide

**Permanent (Truth):**
- `documentation/architecture/adr/` - Why we built it this way
- `documentation/architecture/contracts/` - How systems interface
- `documentation/tech_stack.md` - What we use

---

## Spec-Driven Development & agent-os Maintenance (Summary)

- **Bug fix** → just code, no ceremony
- **Tactical change** → `/bmad-bmm-quick-spec` + `/bmad-bmm-quick-dev`
- **Architectural change** → full BMAD pipeline (detect phase from ticket artifacts)
- **After code changes architecture** → run `/push-adr` to update `agent-os/`
- **Ticket closing** → promote ADRs to `documentation/architecture/adr/`

**Need details?** → [Spec-Driven Development Workflow](documentation/process/spec-driven-development-workflow.md) covers:
- BMAD phase detection: which signals map to which BMAD skills
- agent-os update rules: when/what/how to update specs after code changes
- `/push-adr` usage and cross-reference requirements
- Promotion workflow from BMAD output → agent-os → global documentation

---

## Self-Correcting Memory

When Claude receives a correction or detects a mistake during a session:

### Severity Assessment

- **Minor** (naming convention, file placement, process step missed): Update the relevant CLAUDE.md with a concise rule. Tell the user: "I'm updating my memory in CLAUDE.md to avoid this in the future."
- **Moderate** (repeated pattern, workflow gap, recurring friction): Propose running `/gab-operationalize` to codify as a skill, agent instruction, or README update.
- **Major** (architectural anti-pattern, security issue, process failure): Propose creating a dedicated skill or process document in `documentation/process/`.

### Rules

- Always tell the user what you're updating and why (no silent changes)
- Place the update at the right scope level: global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (cross-project), project CLAUDE.md (project-specific), or repo CLAUDE.md (repo-specific)
- Keep updates concise — one rule per lesson, not paragraphs
- Reference the context that triggered the correction (e.g., "Learned from SPV-3 session: ...")
- Never duplicate — check if a similar rule already exists before adding

---

## DRY Principle

This file defines core rules and decision criteria inline. Detailed procedures are extracted to satellite files in `documentation/process/`:
- [Core Workflow Guide](documentation/process/core-workflow-guide.md) — capture, synthesize, formalize, contracts, deployment ordering
- [Ticket Initialization Guide](documentation/process/ticket-initialization-guide.md) — epic setup, REPO_MAPPING, STATUS_SNAPSHOT operationalization
- [Agent Report Output Standards](documentation/process/agent-report-output-standards.md) — report generation, naming, Jira folder, /status-index
- [Spec-Driven Development Workflow](documentation/process/spec-driven-development-workflow.md) — BMAD phases, agent-os maintenance, promotion

Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files only add context-specific nuances, not repeat these rules.
```

```text
---
name: test-harness-driver
description: Drives the SPV-3 local test harness — builds images, starts services, runs integration tests, diagnoses failures, and fixes issues. Sequential gate pipeline with auto-remediation.
tools: Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, AskUserQuestion
model: sonnet
---

# Test Harness Driver Agent

You are an integration test orchestration agent for the SPV-3 local test harness. You drive the harness through a sequential gate pipeline: **Preflight → Build → Boot → Test → Diagnose → Report**.

Stop at any gate failure that you cannot auto-remediate. Persist reports after each phase.

---

## Harness Location

```
~/Developer/supervisr-ai/project-management/tools/test-harness/
```

Scripts: `scripts/build-images.sh`, `scripts/start.sh`, `scripts/stop.sh`, `scripts/test-spv3-flow.sh`
Compose: `docker-compose.yml`
Mock: `mocks/retell-ai/`

Reports go to: `~/Developer/supervisr-ai/project-management/tickets/SPV-3/reports/status/`

---

## Phase 0: Preflight

**Goal:** Verify all prerequisites before attempting anything.

### Checks

1. **Docker daemon running:**
   - `docker info &>/dev/null`
   - If not running → STOP: "Docker Desktop is not running. Start it and re-run."

2. **GCP credentials present:**
   - `ls ~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json`
   - If missing → STOP: "Run `gcloud auth application-default login` first."

3. **Maven + JDK available:**
   - `mvn --version` — verify JDK 21+
   - If missing → STOP: "Maven + JDK 21 required. Install via `brew install maven` or SDKMAN."

4. **Service source dirs exist:**
   - Check all 5 dirs under `~/Developer/supervisr-ai/app/micro-services/`:
     - `lead-lifecycle-service/`
     - `retell-service/`
     - `compliance-ers/`
     - `supervisor-query-service/`
     - `supervisor-compliance-engine/`
   - If any missing → STOP: "Service directory not found: {path}"

5. **Port conflicts:**
   - Check ports 8080, 8082, 8085, 9000, 9001, 9002, 4010 are free
   - `lsof -i :{port} -sTCP:LISTEN`
   - If occupied → WARN user, suggest `scripts/stop.sh` or manual kill

**Gate:** All 5 checks pass → proceed to Phase 1.

---

## Phase 1: Build

**Goal:** Build Docker images for all 5 services + 1 mock.

### Steps

1. Run `scripts/build-images.sh` (or `--force` if user requests rebuild)
2. Monitor output for failures
3. If a JIB build fails:
   - Read the Maven error output
   - Common fixes: missing dependency → `mvn clean install -DskipTests` in the failing service dir
   - Retry once after fix
4. Verify all 6 images exist:
   - `docker images --format '{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}' | grep ':local'`
   - Expected: `lead-lifecycle-service:local`, `retell-service:local`, `web-ers:local`, `query-service:local`, `supervisor-compliance-engine:local`

**Gate:** All 5 service images + mock builds successfully → proceed to Phase 2.

---

## Phase 2: Boot

**Goal:** Start all services and verify they reach healthy state.

### Steps

1. Run `scripts/start.sh`
2. If any service fails to start:
   - `docker compose -f tools/test-harness/docker-compose.yml logs {service-name}` — read last 100 lines
   - Common issues:
     - **PubSub connection refused:** EmulatorConfiguration not picking up `PUBSUB_EMULATOR_HOST` → check application-local.yml
     - **Port already in use:** Another instance running → `scripts/stop.sh` then retry
     - **OOM / slow start:** Increase Docker memory or extend healthcheck `start_period`
     - **Datastore auth:** GCP credentials not mounted → check `GCLOUD_CONFIG_DIR` env var
   - Attempt fix and retry (max 2 retries)

3. Verify EmulatorConfiguration ran:
   - `docker compose logs lead-lifecycle 2>&1 | grep -i "emulator is enabled"`
   - `docker compose logs eqs 2>&1 | grep -i "emulator is enabled"`
   - `docker compose logs compliance-ers 2>&1 | grep -i "autogenerate"`
   - If topics not created → diagnose and report

**Gate:** All 7 containers healthy + PubSub topics provisioned → proceed to Phase 3.

---

## Phase 3: Test

**Goal:** Run the end-to-end flow test.

### Steps

1. Run `scripts/test-spv3-flow.sh`
2. Capture full output
3. If any step fails:
   - **Step 1 (health):** Service crashed after boot → check logs, go back to Phase 2
   - **Step 3 (ingest lead):** GraphQL mutation wrong → read the actual schema from service, fix the mutation in the test script
   - **Step 4-5 (tick/dial):** Endpoint path wrong → check lead-lifecycle controller mappings
   - **Step 6 (verify call):** Retell mock not called → check retell-service logs for outbound call attempt, verify `RETELL_API_BASE_URL` points to mock
   - **Step 7 (callback):** Webhook delivery failed → check retell-service webhook endpoint path
   - **Step 9 (EQS query):** Data not propagated → check PubSub topic flow, verify EQS subscription received events

4. For GraphQL errors: read the actual `.graphqls` schema files from the service source to correct payloads

**Gate:** All 9 steps pass → proceed to Report.

---

## Phase 4: Report

**Goal:** Write a test run report.

### Output

Write report to `tickets/SPV-3/reports/status/test-harness-run-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md`:

```markdown
# Test Harness Run — {date}

## Result: {PASS|FAIL}

## Phase Results
| Phase | Status | Duration | Notes |
|-------|--------|----------|-------|
| Preflight | ... | ... | ... |
| Build | ... | ... | ... |
| Boot | ... | ... | ... |
| Test | ... | ... | ... |

## Service Health
| Service | Image | Container Status | PubSub Topics Created |
|---------|-------|-----------------|----------------------|

## Test Steps
| Step | Description | Result | Details |
|------|-------------|--------|---------|

## Issues Found & Fixes Applied
...

## Remaining Issues
...
```

---

## Commands

The user can invoke you with context:

- **`run all`** — Full pipeline: Preflight → Build → Boot → Test → Report
- **`build`** — Preflight + Build only
- **`boot`** — Preflight + Boot only (assumes images exist)
- **`test`** — Test only (assumes services are running)
- **`diagnose`** — Read all container logs, check PubSub state, report issues
- **`stop`** — Run `scripts/stop.sh`
- **`rebuild`** — Force rebuild all images (`--force`) then boot

If no command given, default to **`run all`**.

---

## Auto-Remediation Rules

1. **Never modify service source code without asking the user first** — only modify test harness files (scripts, compose, mocks)
2. **Max 2 retry attempts per phase** — if still failing after 2 fixes, stop and report
3. **Always capture logs before attempting a fix** — append to the report
4. **If a fix requires changing a service's application-local.yml or Java code**, describe the change and ask user permission before applying
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

---

# Lead Lifecycle Service - Engineering Reference

Multi-tenant lead lifecycle management service with compliance engine integration.

## Quick Start

See [README.md](README.md) for setup and deployment instructions.

## Architecture Overview

**Service responsibilities:** Multi-tenant lead state management, disposition processing, call request publishing, suppression list management, terminal lead cleanup.

**Key architectural pattern:** ERS/EQS pipeline
- Real-time lead state stored in Google Cloud Datastore
- State changes streamed to ERS (Event Receiver Service) for historical tracking
- Historical queries delegated to EQS (Event Query Service)

**Tech stack:** Java 21, Spring Boot 3.4.5, Netflix DGS 8.7.1, Google Cloud Datastore, Pub/Sub

**Configuration externalization:** Customer configuration is split into 3 entities (`PartnerConfiguration`, `LeadSourceConfiguration`, `PhonePoolEntry`). Writes go through Pub/Sub→compliance-ers (ERS). Reads go through Apollo Gateway→supervisor-query-service (EQS). Local `CustomerConfig` entity has been deleted.

### Core Domain Model

**Lead Lifecycle States:**
- `PENDING` → `CALLING` → `CONTACTED` or `DROPPED`
- Transitions driven by disposition events from compliance engine

**Disposition Processing:**
- Tag-based system: Pub/Sub events contain `call_disposition:*` tags
- `TagMappingService` extracts disposition from tags (standard mapping; customer overrides planned via LeadSourceConfiguration)
- Terminal dispositions (e.g., `DO_NOT_CALL`, `AGE_NOT_ELIGIBLE`) → lead marked `DROPPED`
- Retry-eligible dispositions (e.g., `VOICEMAIL`, `NO_AGENT_AVAILABLE`) → exponential backoff

**Multi-tenancy:**
- Every operation scoped to `customerId` (extracted from JWT `o8/client` claim)
- All Datastore queries filtered by customer ID
- `CustomerContext` injected via `JwtUtil`

### Key Components

| Component | Responsibility |
|-----------|---------------|
| `LeadService` | Core business logic (create, update, disposition) |
| `DispositionEventSubscriber` | Processes Pub/Sub disposition events |
| `TagMappingService` | Maps compliance tags → `CallDisposition` enum |
| `LeadDialerJob` | Scheduled job: finds ready leads, publishes call requests |
| `TerminalLeadCleanupJob` | Scheduled job: deletes terminal leads after grace period |
| `CallRequestPublisher` | Publishes lead call requests to Pub/Sub |
| `ConfigEventPublisher` | Publishes config entity events (YADTO) to Pub/Sub→ERS |
| `SuppressionService` | DNC list management (CSV import/export) |
| `AvailabilityService` | Checks partner availability for call transfers |
| `PartnerConfigurationClient` | Reads partner config from Apollo Gateway (cached) |
| `LeadSourceConfigurationClient` | Reads lead source config from Apollo Gateway (not yet consumed) |
| `PhonePoolClient` | Reads phone pool entries from Apollo Gateway (not yet consumed) |
| `AdminDataFetcher` | GraphQL mutations for managing all 3 config entity types |

For complete architecture: [agent-os/specs/architecture/](agent-os/specs/architecture/)

For API contracts: [agent-os/specs/api-contracts/](agent-os/specs/api-contracts/)

## Package Structure

```
com.supervisr.leadlifecycle/
├── domain/              # Core domain models and enums
│   ├── CallDisposition  # Enum with terminal vs retry-eligible logic
│   ├── LeadStatus       # PENDING → CALLING → CONTACTED/DROPPED
│   ├── CustomerContext  # Multi-tenant context (from JWT)
│   └── config/          # Externalized configuration models
│       ├── PartnerConfiguration
│       ├── LeadSourceConfiguration
│       └── PhonePoolEntry
├── persistence/
│   ├── entities/        # Datastore entities (@Entity)
│   ├── repositories/    # Spring Data Datastore repositories
│   └── dao/             # Data access objects (query logic)
├── services/            # Business logic layer
│   ├── LeadService      # Core lead operations
│   ├── TagMappingService # Disposition tag parsing
│   └── SuppressionService # DNC list management
├── graphql/             # Netflix DGS data fetchers
│   ├── LeadDataFetcher
│   ├── AdminDataFetcher # Config entity mutations (partner, lead source, phone pool)
│   └── SuppressionDataFetcher
├── events/              # Pub/Sub event handling
│   └── DispositionEventSubscriber
├── publishers/          # Pub/Sub publishers
│   ├── CallRequestPublisher    # Lead call requests
│   └── ConfigEventPublisher    # Config entity events (YADTO → ERS)
├── jobs/                # Scheduled tasks (@Scheduled)
│   ├── LeadDialerJob    # Batch lead dialing
│   └── TerminalLeadCleanupJob
├── clients/             # External service clients
│   ├── ComplianceErsClient          # Streams lead events to ERS
│   ├── PartnerConfigurationClient   # Reads partner config via Apollo Gateway
│   ├── LeadSourceConfigurationClient # Reads lead source config via Apollo Gateway
│   └── PhonePoolClient              # Reads phone pool via Apollo Gateway
├── configuration/       # Spring configuration
│   ├── SecurityConfig   # OAuth2 JWT validation
│   ├── JwtUtil          # CustomerContext extraction
│   ├── DatastoreConfig  # Multi-tenant namespace config
│   ├── GraphQlClientConfig # Apollo Gateway HttpGraphQlClient bean
│   └── CacheConfig      # @EnableCaching for @Cacheable support
└── utils/               # Utilities
    └── PhoneNormalizationUtil # E.164 phone formatting
```

**Key patterns:**
- GraphQL is the ONLY API layer (no REST endpoints)
- All operations inject `CustomerContext` via `JwtUtil.extractCustomerContext()`
- All Datastore queries scoped to `customerId` for multi-tenancy
- ERS streaming: every state change → `ComplianceErsClient.publishLeadEvent()`
- Config reads: `*Client` → Apollo Gateway → EQS materialized views (cached with `@Cacheable`)
- Config writes: `AdminDataFetcher` → `ConfigEventPublisher` → Pub/Sub → compliance-ers

## Coding Standards

This project uses **progressive disclosure** for standards — load only what you need.

### Load Relevant Standards

Use `/inject-standards` to intelligently load standards based on your task:

```bash
# Load all standards (full context for architecture work)
/inject-standards

# Load specific domains when working on features
/inject-standards graphql security testing
/inject-standards disposition pubsub performance

# Load specific standards
/inject-standards graphql/api-design
```

**Available standards:** [agent-os/standards/index.yml](agent-os/standards/index.yml)

### Key Standards

- **Code Organization**: Package structure, layered architecture
- **GraphQL**: API design, schema patterns, no REST endpoints
- **Security**: Multi-tenancy, JWT extraction, customer isolation
- **Disposition**: Tag-based system, status transitions, exponential backoff
- **Pub/Sub**: Event patterns, subscribers, publishers, error handling
- **Testing**: Unit test structure, naming patterns, coverage requirements
- **Code Style**: Self-documenting code, method extraction, Boolean naming
- **Performance**: Caching, query optimization, Pub/Sub tuning
- **Debugging**: Common issues, troubleshooting guide, monitoring

## Environment Variables

```bash
# GCP
GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT=your-gcp-project
DATASTORE_PROJECT_ID=your-gcp-project
DATASTORE_NAMESPACE=lead_lifecycle

# Pub/Sub
PUBSUB_PROJECT_ID=your-gcp-project
DISPOSITION_EVENTS_SUBSCRIPTION=compliance-interaction-reports-sub
CALL_REQUESTS_TOPIC=lead-call-requests

# Auth
AUTH0_DOMAIN_URL=https://your-auth0-domain.auth0.com/

# ERS Integration
ERS_BASE_URL=https://compliance-ers.example.com

# Apollo Gateway (config reads via EQS)
APOLLO_GATEWAY_URL=https://apollo-gateway.example.com/graphql

# Spring
SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod
```

## Common Development Patterns

### Adding a New GraphQL Query/Mutation

1. Update GraphQL schema: `src/main/resources/schema/lead.graphqls`
2. Regenerate types: `mvn graphqlcodegen:generate`
3. Implement data fetcher in `graphql/` package (annotate with `@DgsQuery` or `@DgsMutation`)
4. Inject `JwtUtil` and extract `CustomerContext` at the start of the resolver
5. Write unit tests following `{Given}{When}{Then}` naming pattern

### Processing Disposition Events

Flow: Compliance Engine → Pub/Sub → `DispositionEventSubscriber` → `TagMappingService` → `LeadService`

1. `DispositionEventSubscriber` receives Pub/Sub message
2. `TagMappingService.extractDispositionFromTags()` maps tags → `CallDisposition`
3. `LeadService.recordDisposition()` updates lead state
4. If terminal: lead marked `DROPPED`, suppression check
5. If retry-eligible: calculate `nextCallAt` with exponential backoff
6. Stream event to ERS for historical tracking

### Managing Configuration Entities

**Write path** (admin creates/updates config):
1. `AdminDataFetcher` receives GraphQL mutation with config data
2. Builds domain object (`PartnerConfiguration`, `LeadSourceConfiguration`, or `PhonePoolEntry`)
3. `ConfigEventPublisher` wraps in YADTO event and publishes to entity-specific Pub/Sub topic
4. compliance-ers subscribes, persists event, and creates/updates materialized view

**Read path** (service needs config):
1. Inject the appropriate client (`PartnerConfigurationClient`, `LeadSourceConfigurationClient`, `PhonePoolClient`)
2. Call the client method — it queries Apollo Gateway via `HttpGraphQlClient`
3. Apollo Gateway routes to supervisor-query-service (EQS) which reads from materialized views
4. Result is cached locally via `@Cacheable` (Caffeine, TTL-based)

**Adding a new config entity:**
1. Create domain model in `domain/config/`
2. Add Pub/Sub topic constant and publish method in `ConfigEventPublisher`
3. Add GraphQL mutation in `AdminDataFetcher`
4. Add client class with `@Cacheable` in `clients/`
5. Configure compliance-ers `application.yaml` with event kind, MV kind, and identifier
6. Add EQS data fetcher in supervisor-query-service

### Multi-Tenant Data Access

**CRITICAL:** All queries MUST filter by `customerId`:

```java
// Extract customer context from JWT
CustomerContext context = jwtUtil.extractCustomerContext();

// Pass to service layer
Lead lead = leadService.getLead(context, leadUuid);

// Repository queries automatically scoped via @Where annotation
```

Never hardcode customer IDs or skip customer context validation.

### Testing Patterns

- **Extract test constants:** Use `private static final` for reused values
- **Test naming:** `{Given}{When}{Then}` (e.g., `terminalDisposition_whenRecordDisposition_thenMarksDropped`)
- **Structure:** `//given //when //then` comments with no extra blank lines
- **One behavior per test:** Each test verifies exactly one condition
- **Helper methods:** Extract common stub setup (e.g., `stubValidationPasses()`)

Example:
```java
@Test
void terminalDisposition_whenRecordDisposition_thenMarksDropped() {
    //given
    Lead lead = createTestLead(LeadStatus.CALLING);
    when(leadDAO.findByUuid(LEAD_UUID, CUSTOMER_ID)).thenReturn(Optional.of(lead));
    //when
    leadService.recordDisposition(context, LEAD_UUID, createDisposition(CallDisposition.DO_NOT_CALL));
    //then
    assertEquals(LeadStatus.DROPPED, lead.getStatus());
}
```

## Debugging & Troubleshooting

### Leads Not Being Dialed

1. Check lead status: `status = PENDING` and `nextCallAt <= now()`
2. Verify partner config via Apollo Gateway: `PartnerConfigurationClient.getPartnerConfiguration()`
3. Check `LeadDialerJob` logs for batch processing
4. Verify partner availability: `AvailabilityService.checkAvailability()`
5. Check suppression list: lead phone might be in DNC list

### Disposition Events Not Processing

1. Check Pub/Sub subscription: `DISPOSITION_EVENTS_SUBSCRIPTION` env var
2. Verify event format contains `call_disposition:*` tag
3. Check `TagMappingService` logs for tag extraction
4. Verify `customerId` and `leadUuid` present in event payload
5. Look for reconciliation tags (events with `reconciliation` tag are skipped)

### GraphQL Authentication Errors

1. Verify Auth0 issuer URL: `AUTH0_DOMAIN_URL` env var
2. Check JWT token contains `o8/client` claim (customer ID)
3. Verify `SecurityConfig` allows `/private/graphql` with authentication
4. Test token decoding: `JwtUtil.extractCustomerContext()`

### Multi-Tenant Data Leaks

**Red flags:**
- Queries without `customerId` filter
- Hardcoded customer IDs in code
- Missing `CustomerContext` parameter in service methods
- Repository methods not using `@Where` annotation

**Prevention:** Every service method signature should include `CustomerContext` as first parameter.

## Development Commands

### Local Development

```bash
# Build (includes GraphQL codegen)
mvn clean compile

# Run locally
mvn spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=local

# Regenerate GraphQL types from schema
mvn graphqlcodegen:generate
```

### Testing

```bash
# Run all tests
mvn test

# Run specific test class
mvn test -Dtest=LeadServiceTest

# Run specific test method
mvn test -Dtest=LeadServiceTest#validDisposition_whenRecordDisposition_thenUpdatesLead
```

### Build & Deploy

```bash
# Package without tests
mvn clean package -DskipTests

# Build and push Docker image to Artifact Registry
mvn compile jib:build -Djib.to.tags=v1.0.0

# Deploy to Cloud Run
gcloud run deploy lead-lifecycle-service \
  --image us-east1-docker.pkg.dev/prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95/are-usea1-docker-standard-backend/lead-lifecycle-service:v1.0.0 \
  --region us-east1

# Publish GraphQL schema to Apollo (requires APOLLO_KEY)
rover subgraph publish SupervisrAI@dev \
  --schema src/main/resources/schema/lead.graphqls \
  --name lead-lifecycle-service \
  --routing-url <cloud-run-url>/private/graphql
```

## Key Configuration

### Exponential Backoff (Disposition Retries)

Non-terminal dispositions trigger exponential backoff for retry:

```yaml
lead:
  defaults:
    baseDelayMinutes: 60      # First retry: 60 minutes
    maxAttempts: 8            # Max 8 attempts
    maxDelayMinutes: 1440     # Cap at 24 hours (no longer grows)

# Retry sequence: 60min, 120min, 240min, 480min, 960min, 1440min, 1440min, 1440min
```

Implemented in: `LeadService.calculateNextAttemptDate()`. Retry config (baseDelayMinutes, maxAttempts, maxDelayMinutes) comes from `PartnerConfiguration` via Apollo Gateway.

### Scheduled Jobs

```yaml
lead:
  dialer:
    batchSize: 5                     # Leads per customer per cycle
    schedulerFixedDelayMs: 30000     # Run every 30 seconds
    staleCallingTimeoutMinutes: 15   # Recover stale CALLING leads
    staleRecoveryDelayMs: 60000      # Check for stale leads every 60 seconds
  cleanup:
    terminalGracePeriodMinutes: 30   # Delete terminal leads after 30 min
    schedulerFixedDelayMs: 300000    # Run every 5 minutes
```

### Java Compatibility

- **Runtime:** Java 21 LTS (supported until Sept 2031)
- **Mockito on Java 21+:** Requires explicit byte-buddy agent via maven-surefire-plugin (configured in pom.xml)
- **All tests pass on Java 21** (49/49)

### Infrastructure (DAC)

Cloud Run deployment is managed via Terraform in the DAC repository:

```
faas/grp-dac/grp-dac-sprvsr/grp-dac-sprvsr-core/dac-sprvsr-core-lead-lifecycle/
```

**IMPORTANT:** Do NOT run terraform commands directly. Changes must be reviewed and applied via CI/CD pipeline.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

Event Query Service - SupervisrAI is a specialized Spring Boot GraphQL service built with Netflix DGS framework that serves domain-specific queries for call and interaction analytics. This service is optimized for the SupervisrAI product domain, focusing on call review, quality monitoring, and supervisor analytics. It acts as an Apollo Federation subgraph, combining with other services through the Apollo Gateway.

### Core Responsibilities
- **Call Analytics Queries**: Expose call and interaction data for analysis
- **Supervisor Metrics**: Provide team and agent performance metrics
- **Quality Monitoring**: Query call quality assessments and feedback
- **Apollo Federation**: Registered as subgraph in Apollo Gateway
- **Data Access**: Query Datastore materialized views for supervisor domain
- **Authentication**: JWT-based access control

## Development Commands

### Build and Test
```bash
# Clean build and run tests
mvn clean test

# Build without tests
mvn clean compile

# Run specific test
mvn test -Dtest=CallAnalyticsDataFetcherTest
```

### Local Development
```bash
# Run the service locally
mvn spring-boot:run

# Run with specific Spring profile
mvn spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=local
```

### GraphQL Development
```bash
# Generate types from GraphQL schema
mvn graphqlcodegen:generate

# Regenerate after schema changes
mvn clean compile graphqlcodegen:generate
```

### Docker and Deployment

#### Build and Push Docker Image

The service uses Maven Jib plugin to build and push Docker images without requiring Docker daemon.

**Complete workflow (tag, build, push):**
```bash
# 1. Check current tags
git fetch --tags
git tag --list --sort=-v:refname | head -5

# 2. Create new tag (increment patch version)
git tag 0.0.X-dev
git push origin 0.0.X-dev

# 3. Build and push image with same version
mvn compile jib:build -Djib.to.tags=0.0.X-dev -DskipTests
```

**Image location:**
```
us-east1-docker.pkg.dev/prj-cmm-n-build-nqzou69e95/are-usea1-docker-standard-backend/query-service:0.0.X-dev
```

**Note:** The Jib plugin is pre-configured in `pom.xml` to push to the correct Artifact Registry repository.

#### Publish Schema to Apollo Gateway

After deploying a new version, publish the GraphQL schema:

```bash
# Publish to SupervisrAI graph (dev environment)
rover subgraph publish SupervisrAI@dev \
  --schema src/main/resources/schema/schema.graphqls \
  --name query-service \
  --routing-url https://run-usce1-query-service-978993959218.us-central1.run.app/graphql
```

**For other environments:**
```bash
# Sandbox
rover subgraph publish SupervisrAI@sandbox \
  --schema src/main/resources/schema/schema.graphqls \
  --name query-service \
  --routing-url https://run-usce1-query-service-SANDBOX_PROJECT.us-central1.run.app/graphql

# UAT
rover subgraph publish SupervisrAI@uat \
  --schema src/main/resources/schema/schema.graphqls \
  --name query-service \
  --routing-url https://run-usce1-query-service-UAT_PROJECT.us-central1.run.app/graphql

# Prod
rover subgraph publish SupervisrAI@prod \
  --schema src/main/resources/schema/schema.graphqls \
  --name query-service \
  --routing-url https://run-usce1-query-service-PROD_PROJECT.us-central1.run.app/graphql
```

## Architecture

### SupervisrAI Domain Focus

This service is specialized for supervisor and quality management workflows:

#### Call Domain
- Call metadata (duration, date, participants, recording)
- Call transcripts and transcript analysis
- Call tags and categories
- Call outcomes and dispositions

#### Interaction Domain
- Customer interactions across channels
- Interaction timeline
- Interaction outcomes
- Interaction metadata

#### Agent Domain
- Agent performance metrics
- Agent call history
- Agent quality scores
- Agent skill information

#### Team Domain
- Team performance aggregates
- Team metrics and trends
- Team member activities
- Team goals and coaching

### API Endpoints
- **GraphQL**: `/graphql` - Main GraphQL endpoint
- **Apollo Federation**: Exposes `_schema` and entity resolvers for federation
- **Health Check**: `/actuator/health` - Spring Boot Actuator

## Event Query Framework Integration

This service uses the **event-query-framework** library for all Datastore access. The framework provides automatic caching, optimized queries, and standardized filter translation.

### Framework Architecture

```
GraphQL Datafetcher
    ↓
Repository (service-specific)
    ↓
EntityQueryService (framework)
    ├→ DatastoreQueryAdapter (filter → GQL conversion)
    ├→ DatastorePojoAdapter (entity → POJO mapping)
    └→ DatastoreProvider (connection + caching)
```

### How to Use the Framework

#### 1. Create Entity POJOs

Entity POJOs are simple classes that mirror Datastore entities. The framework uses reflection to map them.

**Requirements:**
- No-arg constructor (Lombok `@NoArgsConstructor`)
- Field names MUST match Datastore property names exactly
- Use standard Java types:
  - `String`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Double`, `Boolean`
  - `Date` for timestamps
  - `List<T>` for arrays
  - Nested POJOs for embedded entities

**Example:**
```java
package com.origin8cares.eventquery.domain.entities;

import lombok.Data;
import lombok.NoArgsConstructor;
import java.util.Date;
import java.util.List;

@Data
@NoArgsConstructor
public class InteractionReportEntity {
    private String id;               // From Datastore Key
    private String organizationId;
    private Date startTime;
    private Date endTime;
    private Integer duration;
    private List<TagEntity> tags;    // Nested entities
    private List<UtteranceEntity> utterances;
}
```

**Location**: `src/main/java/com/origin8cares/eventquery/domain/entities/`

#### 2. Create Mappers

Mappers convert framework POJOs to GraphQL generated types. This bridges Datastore data to your API schema.

**Pattern:**
```java
@Service
public class InteractionReportMapper {
    public InteractionReport toGraphQL(InteractionReportEntity entity) {
        return InteractionReport.newBuilder()
            .id(entity.getId())
            .organizationId(entity.getOrganizationId())
            .startTime(toLocalDate(entity.getStartTime()))
            .tags(entity.getTags().stream()
                .map(this::mapTag)
                .collect(Collectors.toList()))
            .build();
    }

    private LocalDate toLocalDate(Date date) {
        return date != null ?
            date.toInstant().atZone(ZoneId.systemDefault()).toLocalDate() : null;
    }
}
```

**Location**: `src/main/java/com/origin8cares/eventquery/mappers/`

#### 3. Create Repositories

Repositories use `EntityQueryService` to query Datastore and map results.

**Pattern:**
```java
@Repository
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class InteractionReportRepository {

    private static final String KIND = "interaction_reports";  // Datastore kind name

    private final EntityQueryService entityQueryService;
    private final InteractionReportMapper mapper;

    public List<InteractionReport> findByFilters(RootFiltersInput filters) {
        // Framework handles filter translation and caching automatically
        List<InteractionReportEntity> entities = entityQueryService.getEntities(
            filters,
            KIND,
            InteractionReportEntity.class
        );

        // Map to GraphQL types
        return entities.stream()
            .map(mapper::toGraphQL)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
    }

    public long countByFilters(RootFiltersInput filters) {
        return entityQueryService.getEntityCount(
            filters,
            KIND,
            InteractionReportEntity.class
        );
    }
}
```

**Location**: `src/main/java/com/origin8cares/eventquery/persistence/`

#### 4. Use in Datafetchers

Datafetchers call repositories - the framework handles everything else.

**Pattern:**
```java
@DgsComponent
public class DatastoreDatafetcher {

    @Autowired
    private InteractionReportRepository repository;

    @DgsQuery
    public InteractionReportResult InteractionReportResults(@InputArgument RootFiltersInput filters) {
        List<InteractionReport> reports = repository.findByFilters(filters);
        long count = repository.countByFilters(filters);

        InteractionReportResult result = new InteractionReportResult();
        result.setResults(reports);
        result.setCount((int) count);
        return result;
    }
}
```

### Framework Features

**Automatic Caching:**
- Framework caches entities per Datastore kind/table
- Cache is updated asynchronously on query
- Reduces Datastore API calls significantly

**Optimized Queries:**
- Step 1: Run KEY-only query (fast, returns only keys)
- Step 2: Check cache for cached entities
- Step 3: Batch fetch missing keys (max 1000 per batch)
- Step 4: Update cache asynchronously

**Filter Translation:**
- Framework automatically converts `RootFiltersInput` to Datastore GQL
- Supports all O8 comparators: `==`, `!=`, `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`, `in`, `not-in`, `array-contains`, `array-contains-any`
- Handles `and`, `or`, `nor` logic
- Supports sorting and pagination

**No Custom Code Needed:**
- ❌ Don't create custom filter translators
- ❌ Don't create custom entity mappers (for Datastore → POJO)
- ❌ Don't manage caching manually
- ✅ Just create: Entity POJOs, Mappers (POJO → GraphQL), Repositories

### Configuration Required

**pom.xml:**
```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.origin8.tools</groupId>
    <artifactId>event-query-framework</artifactId>
    <version>1.0.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
</dependency>
```

**ApplicationConfig.java:**
```java
@Bean
DatastoreProvider datastoreProvider() {
    return () -> {
        if (datastoreNamespaceProvider().get() != null) {
            return DatastoreOptions.newBuilder()
                    .setProjectId(projectId)
                    .setNamespace(datastoreNamespaceProvider().get())
                    .build()
                    .getService();
        } else {
            return DatastoreOptions.newBuilder()
                    .setProjectId(projectId)
                    .build()
                    .getService();
        }
    };
}
```

**application.yml:**
```yaml
application:
  datastore:
    namespace: ${DATASTORE_NAMESPACE:#{null}}
    projectId: ${DATASTORE_PROJECT_ID}
```

### Best Practices

1. **Entity Naming**: Use `*Entity` suffix (e.g., `InteractionReportEntity`)
2. **Mapper Naming**: Use `*Mapper` suffix (e.g., `InteractionReportMapper`)
3. **Repository Naming**: Use `*Repository` suffix (e.g., `InteractionReportRepository`)
4. **Datastore Kind**: Use snake_case matching Datastore (e.g., `interaction_reports`)
5. **Field Names**: MUST match Datastore exactly - framework uses reflection
6. **Nested Entities**: Create separate entity classes for embedded objects
7. **Date Handling**: Use `java.util.Date` in entities, convert to `LocalDate` in mappers
8. **Enums**: Store as String in Datastore/entities, convert to enum in mappers

### Framework Auto-Configuration

The framework auto-configures via Spring Boot:
- `EntityQueryService` - Core query service
- `DatastoreQueryAdapter` - Filter translation
- `DatastorePojoAdapter` - Entity → POJO mapping

**No manual wiring needed** - just inject `EntityQueryService` in repositories.

### Query Examples

#### Simple Query
```graphql
query {
  InteractionReportResults(filters: {
    and: [
      { key: "organizationId", comparator: "==", value: "org-123" }
    ]
  }) {
    count
    results {
      id
      startTime
      duration
    }
  }
}
```

#### Complex Query with Sorting
```graphql
query {
  RuleResults(filters: {
    and: [
      { key: "organizationId", comparator: "==", value: "org-123" },
      { key: "isActive", comparator: "==", value: true }
    ],
    sort: [{ key: "createdAt", order: -1 }],
    limit: 10
  }) {
    count
    results {
      id
      name
      displayName
      isActive
    }
  }
}
```

### Important Notes

1. **Date Handling**: Framework uses `java.util.Date`, mappers convert to `LocalDate`
2. **Field Names**: Entity field names MUST match Datastore property names exactly
3. **Enum Handling**: Store as String in entities, convert to enum in mappers
4. **Nested Objects**: Create separate entity classes for embedded Datastore entities
5. **Caching**: Framework caches automatically - no manual cache management needed

### Key Components

#### GraphQL Schema
- File: `src/main/resources/schema/schema.graphqls`
- Defines call, interaction, agent, and team types
- Federation directives for Apollo Gateway
- Custom scalars for complex types
- Code generation creates Java types automatically

#### Data Fetchers
- Located: `src/main/java/com/origin8cares/eventquery/graphql/datafetchers/`
- Use repositories to query data
- Return GraphQL result types

#### Repository Layer
- Located: `src/main/java/com/origin8cares/eventquery/persistence/`
- Use `EntityQueryService` from framework
- Return GraphQL types via mappers

#### Authentication
- JWT token validation via OAuth2 resource server
- Auth0 integration for token validation
- Role-based access control (Supervisor, Admin, Agent roles)
- Tenant isolation for multi-tenant scenarios

### External Integrations
- **Google Cloud Datastore**: Materialized views persistence (via framework)
- **Apollo Federation Gateway**: GraphQL schema registration
- **Auth0**: OAuth2 token validation

## Key Configuration Files

- `pom.xml`: Maven dependencies, Jib plugin, GraphQL code generation
- `src/main/resources/application.yml`: Spring Boot and DGS configuration
- `src/main/resources/application-local.yml`: Local development overrides
- `src/main/resources/schema/schema.graphqls`: GraphQL schema definition for SupervisrAI domain

## GraphQL Schema Structure

The schema follows this domain model:

```graphql
# Root Query Types
type Query {
  # Interaction queries
  InteractionReportResults(filters: RootFiltersInput): InteractionReportResult!
  
  # Rule queries
  RuleResults(filters: RootFiltersInput): RuleResult!
}

# Key Types
type InteractionReport {
  id: ID!
  organizationId: ID!
  startTime: Date!
  endTime: Date
  duration: Int
  supervisor: String
  disposition: String
  tags: [Tag!]!
  utterances: [Utterance!]!
  speakers: [Speaker!]!
}

type Rule {
  id: ID!
  organizationId: ID!
  name: String!
  displayName: String
  category: TagCategory
  isActive: Boolean!
  isDeleted: Boolean!
  createdAt: Date!
  updatedAt: Date!
  tags: [TagForRule!]!
  parentComplianceRuleIds: [String!]!
}
```

## Code Generation

- GraphQL types automatically generated from schema
- Generated code in `target/generated-sources`
- Types available for data fetcher implementation
- Regenerate after any schema changes: `mvn graphqlcodegen:generate`

## Testing

### Running Tests
```bash

# With coverage
